data.title: Amazon: Cheaper Than You Think
data.description:
AMZN stock’s record Prime Day

My last work on Amazon stock (NASDAQ:AMZN) focused on its partnership with Oracle. That article, entitled “Amazon: Oracle Partnership Highlights Margin Expansion Potential,” argued for a buy rating on considerations of its AWS' leading position and AI opportunities. More specifically, I argued that “AMZN’s recently announced partnership with Oracle highlights AWS' leading position and AI opportunities. Its AWS segment features a far higher margin than retail (~35% vs. ~4.5% in my estimate), thus offering the potential to both drive rapid EPS growth and also expand margins considerably."

However, in that article, I also express a concern regarding the uncertainties surrounding its retail segment. Since that writing, a significant event for the company was its recent Prime Big Deal Days. More specifics of the event are quoted below. The success of this event signals to me the recovery of its retail business.

SA news: Amazon said Thursday that its Prime Big Deal Days was the biggest October shopping event for the company as more Prime members shopped compared to last year. The company said Prime members around the world saved more than $1 billion across deals, including on seasonal merchandise and gifts. The company said in the three weeks leading up to Prime Big Deal Days, "a record number of customers" worldwide signed up for Prime compared to last year’s event.

This development has led me to examine the profit of its retail segment and also the stock’s valuation. In the remainder of this article, you will see that my findings are two-fold. First, I see several ongoing catalysts that could first drive up the profitability of the retail segment and also the overall EPS growth for the company. These catalysts have remedied my earlier concern over its retail segment. Second, the current accounting earnings dramatically underestimate its true owners' earnings (by more than 20% in my estimate) due to the large-growth capex investments made recently. As a result, the stock’s P/E based on owners’ earnings are significantly below that based on accounting earnings.

Based on these considerations, I now rate the stock as a Strong Buy, an upgrade from my earlier Buy rating.

AMZN stock: Retail segment in focus

Let me start with the profitability outlook of the retail segment. I consider the success of its recent Prime Day another demonstration of the convenience and attractive pricing of Amazon’s e-commerce. I expect the retail business continues to do well in future for this well-established edge.

The retail operations have faced some challenges in recent years as elaborated on in my earlier article. The segment went through some major restructuring efforts in recent years to boost profitability and growth in response. These efforts are effective, in my view. As an example, retail operating margins climbed to 5.0% last quarter, helping to drive up the overall margin for the business to the highest level in the past decade (see the next chart below). The segment also expanded many of its key features, such as same-day delivery.

Looking ahead, I continue to see a multitude of opportunities for the company to further improve profitability and achieve growth on this front. A few top catalysts on my list are its ongoing initiatives into healthcare, vehicle sales, and also further cost-saving potential. AMZN recently rolled out its One Medical primary health services for a small fee. Online vehicle sales have also become a new feature starting with Hyundai recently. The company’s investments in recent years to improve its freight and fulfillment efficiencies are most focused on its domestic operations. I see plenty of opportunities to implement these cost-saving actions overseas.

AMZN stock: Growth outlook and valuation

Besides the potential in its established retail segment, the company also enjoys tremendous growth opportunities on the burgeoning cloud and AI front. These opportunities were the focus of my last article, so I won’t further elaborate here anymore.

The profitability and growth potential in both is retail and cloud segments adds to a rapid growth curve ahead, as reflected in the following consensus EPS estimates. According to the estimates, AMZN’s EPS is expected to increase from $4.73 in FY 2024 to 10.79 in five years (i.e., FY 2028). This represents a compound annual growth rate of almost 22%.

The forward P/E ratios for AMZN stock are expected to decline in tandem over the next few years. The current FWD P/E is estimated to be 39.54 based on the consensus EPS. This figure is expected to decrease to 32 next year and to about 25x only in two years.

Next, I will explain why the stock is considerably cheaper than such accounting P/E suggests.

AMZN stock: Accounting EPS vs. owners earnings

As aforementioned, due to the large growth capex investments AMZN made recently (largely in AI-related projects), its accounting earnings are an underestimate of its owners earnings.

The analysis of the owners earnings requires us A) to split its capex into maintenance and growth capex, and B) then add the growth part back into earnings because this part is optional. In my analysis here, I used the following approach described in Greenwald’s book entitled Value Investing. I highly recommend the book for readers interested in this topic and here I will quote the gist for ease of reference:

The growth part should actually be considered part of the owners’ earnings because it can be returned to the owners if the owners decide not to grow the business anymore – a key insight that investors like Buffett have recognized. The maintenance capex and growth capex in this work is estimated in these steps. First, calculate the ratio of PPE (properties, plants, and equipment) to sales for each of the five prior years and find the average. We use this to indicate the dollars of PPE it takes to support each dollar of sales. We then multiply this ratio by the growth (or decrease) in sales dollars the company has achieved in the current year. The result of that calculation is growth capex. We then subtract it from total capex to arrive at maintenance capex.

Following this approach, the next chart shows my estimates of AMZN’s owners earnings in recent years. As seen, its owners earnings have consistently exceeded both its accounting EPS and also free cash flow (“FCF”) in the past, a hallmark of a growth stock. In 2024, due to the large growth-oriented capex, my estimate is that its owners earnings are about 20% above its accounting EPS.

Thus, AMZN’s valuation is not as high as on the surface. Based on my estimates of its owners earnings, its FY1 P/E is about 32.9x, vs. about 40x in terms of accounting P/E.

Other risks and final thoughts

In terms of downside risks, AMZN’s AI initiatives are in an early stage. Investors’ clamoring for the “all things AI” scenario could take a pause and cause temporary stock price corrections. As an example, in the past earnings season, many analysts questioned the potential returns on the industry’s massive AI investments (including AMZN’s). I'm optimistic of the long-term potential of AI-related technologies to boost productivity (they are already boosting my productivity now). However, I'm fully aware that these investments face uncertainties and could cause short-term profit headwinds.

On the retail front, AMZN faces intense competition from other retail giants like Walmart (WMT) and Target (TGT) as well as recent entrants like PDD's (PDD) TEMU. In particular, TEMU could be a disruptive force in several ways. TEMU features ultra-low pricing. TEMU's business model, which involves direct sourcing from manufacturers and minimal markups, allows it to offer products at significantly lower prices than Amazon. This is particularly appealing to budget-conscious consumers. TEMU's gamified shopping experience also integrates shopping and social features in an effective way, potentially driving higher customer loyalty and retention.

All told, my overall conclusion is that the positives are the prevailing force in the next few years and make AMZN a compelling investment under current conditions. To recap, my early Buy rating was largely due to the concern of its retail uncertainties. However, recent developments, especially the Prime day success and margin expansion, have dispelled that concern. A closer look at its capex and earnings also reveals that its valuation multiples to be significantly lower than what’s on the surface. These developments have led me to upgrade my rating to Strong Buy.

data.title: Upwork: A Small Bet Activist Pressure Will Succeed
data.description:
Shares of online gig marketplace Upwork Inc. (NASDAQ:UPWK) were down 37% year-to-date when activist investor Engine Capital announced a 3.5% stake, sparking a 10% rally on September 13, 2024. The dreadful share price performance was in spite of the company raising its bottom-line guidance twice in 2024, as concerns about a weak macro backdrop and the threat of AI obsolescence weigh. Featuring low P/E and EV/EBITDA multiples with significant improvement in take rates despite the tough environment, Upwork merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

Upwork Inc. is a San Fransisco-based provider of the world’s largest online platform for freelance talent as measured by gross services volume (GSV). Its marketplace enables businesses to find, hire, and pay professionals for projects covering over 10,000 skills across more than 125 work categories in over 180 countries. The company was formed in 2014 through the merger of Elance (founded in 1998) and oDesk (2003), two prior rivals in the freelance space. Upwork changed to its current moniker in 2015 and went public in 2018, raising net proceeds of $109.4 million at $15 per share. The stock trades just below $10.50 a share, translating to a market cap of just under $1.4 billion.

Business Model

The company generates a majority of its revenue from talent service fees charged to freelancers, which is a percentage of their billings to clients, as well as client marketplace fees, which are charged to the job posters on a per-transaction basis. The talent service fees used to be tiered from 5% to 20% until May 2023, when a simplified 10% flat fee replaced the prior structure. Also included in Upwork’s fee alteration was the increase in the number of virtual tokens (known as Connects) required for freelancers to bid on projects and the introduction of a contract initiation fee to clients.

Revenue Disaggregation

The notional value of all freelance transactions conducted on its platform is the aforementioned GSV, which totaled $4.1 billion in FY23. Approximately 26% of that GSV was generated from U.S. talent, followed by India and the Philippines. Looking from the other perspective, 69% of its FY23 GSV was derived from U.S. clients. In other words, the company’s largest source of revenue is American businesses outsourcing work overseas.

The company further disaggregates its revenue into Marketplace and Enterprise.

In addition to the aforementioned talent service and client marketplace fees, Marketplace also generates revenue from advertising products on its platform, talent memberships, and foreign exchange fees for transactions in currencies other than the dollar, as well as premium offerings such as Upwork Payroll. Owing in large measure to the changes (essentially price increases) that were implemented in 2Q23, Marketplace generated FY23 revenue of $586.1 million, up 13% from FY22, even though its GSV was up only 1% at $4.1 billion, meaning its take rate increased from 13.8% in FY22 to 15.4% in FY23. At its core, the performance in FY23 was a demonstration of Upwork’s ability to leverage the scale of its marketplace and strong talent retention into greater returns.

Marketplace comprised 85% of the company’s FY23 top line, with the balance coming from Enterprise solutions, consisting of all Marketplace products plus dedicated account managers, access to expert-vetted talent, and payment terms flexibility, amongst other services. Upwork also provides a Managed Services functionality that falls under Enterprise, where it acts as the defacto project manager, engaging talent directly to conduct the needed tasks. Seen as a source of future growth, Enterprise generated FY23 revenue of $103.0 million, up 3% from FY22.

The Playing Field

The market for skilled gig services, at least in the U.S. is substantial, with competitor Fiverr estimating that there were 6.3 million skilled independent workers in creative, technical, and professional services earning income of $247 billion in 2021. Most of the placement for these jobs still operate off line through staffing service providers such as The Adecco Group (AXEHY), Randstad (OTCPK:RANJF), Allegis Group, and Robert Half International (RHI), as well as online platforms such as Recruit Holding’s (OTCPK:RCRRF) Indeed.com.

Upwork’s most direct competitor is Fiverr (FVRR), a company that is broader-based, focusing on lower priced, shorter-term freelance opportunities. It charges a client fee of 5.5% and a talent services fee equal to 20% of the GSV. Fiverr generated FY23 non-GAAP net income of $80.4 million ($1.95 per share) on revenue of $361.4 million for a non-GAAP net margin of 22%, comparing favorably to Upwork’s non-GAAP net income of $70.5 million ($0.52 per share) on revenue of $689.1 million for a non-GAAP net margin of 10% – the difference being the higher talent service fee at Fiverr.

Share Price Performance

After bottoming out a $5.14 a share in the throes of the March 2020 selloff, Upwork’s stock was bid substantially higher as it was rightly perceived as a beneficiary of the pandemic, soaring 1,104% to a peak of $61.90 a share in October 2021. However, at that juncture, shares of UPWK were trading at an unsustainable 12.7 times FY22 forecasted revenue of $625 million, from which the company was expected to lose $0.10 a share (non-GAAP). Although management nearly delivered on its forecast (generating a loss of only $0.06 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $618.3 million), with the advent of inflation and a rotation out of high-growth, no-profit concerns, Upwork’s share price collapsed 82% by February 2023 – the month in which provided its FY22 financial results – and nearly round-tripped to $6.56 a share after reporting its 1Q23 financials in May 2023, the month in which management implemented its new pricing structure.

Two trading sessions after the company reported its 2Q23 financials, which bested expectations largely due to the implementation of its pricing strategy for half the quarter and effective cost cutting measures, shares of UPWK rebounded 142% to a 2023 high of $15.88.

Buoyed by its 150 basis point year-over-year take rate improvement in 4Q23 to 17.2% (and 120 basis points since the introduction of its new pricing model), management initially guided FY24 to non-GAAP EPS of $0.79 and Adj. EBITDA of $130.0 million on revenue of $770.0 million, representing 52%, 78%, and 12% improvements over FY23, respectively. All forecasts were based on range midpoints. Those estimates were all revised upward (to $0.90, $145 million, and $776 million) after Upwork’s’ 1Q24 financials in May 2024.

2Q24 Financials & FY24 Outlook

However, a downward top-line revision as part of its 2Q24 financial report pressured shares of UPWK. On August 7, 2024 The company posted net income of $0.26 a share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $40.8 million on revenue of $193.1 million versus $0.10 a share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $14.4 million on revenue of $168.6 million in 2Q23, representing increases of 160%, 183%, and 15%, respectively. The bottom line was $0.03 better than Street expectations while the top line matched consensus. Total take rate increased 290 basis points year-over-year to 19.2% while active clients increased 6% year-over-year to 868,000, although down slightly from 872,000 in 1Q24. That said, GSV declined 3% to $1.0 billion and GSV per active client fell 5% year-over-year to $4,745.

The take rate performance was primarily a function of the flat 10% fee pricing, which had its anniversary in 2Q24, as well as the impact of ads and other monetization products, suggesting the momentum in this metric should slow going forward.

Based on share repurchases in the quarter, management upped its FY24 non-GAAP earnings forecast to $0.92 a share and maintained its previously upwardly revised Adj. EBITDA guidance at $145 million. However, due to a challenging macroeconomic backdrop, Upwork lowered is revenue projection from $776 million to $740 million. The company said it was able to maintain the Adj. EBITDA guide due to solid progress on corralling expenses. The market elected to focus on the top-line revision, selling shares of UPWK down 10% to $9.37 in the subsequent trading session, a level around which they had more or less flatlined until September 13, 2024.

It was on that date that activist investor Engine Capital disclosed a 3.5% stake in Upwork, publishing a letter to its board, suggesting that the company needed to fix “foundational issues” menacing its platform that “risks becoming increasingly irrelevant”, while insinuating that the CEO and the board of directors should be replaced due to a “lack of strategic clarity and focus”. Engine also cited Upwork underperforming its closest rival Fiverr. Down 37% year-to-date (versus Fiverr down 10% YTD), largely on fears that artificial intelligence would significantly diminish the need for freelancers, shares of UPWK rallied 10% that day to $10.24. The company acknowledged receipt of Engine’s correspondence but did not respond to its specific criticisms.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

If anything, the entry of Engine into the mix highlights Upwork’s staunch cash generation, having produced $42.9 million from operations in 1H24. Its balance sheet reflected cash and securities of $497.7 million against 0.25% convertible debt of $357.0 million (due 2026) as of June 30, 2024. The company repurchased $100 million of stock at an average price of $12.38 a share in 1H24 but has no current authorization. Upwork does not pay a dividend.

Despite the concerns of Engine and the existential threat of AI, the Street leans positive on the company’s future, featuring eight buy or outperform ratings against three holds. On average, they expect Upwork to earn $0.83 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $740.7 million in FY24, followed by $1.07 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $784.4 million in FY25.

Verdict:

The company’s top-line ‘woes’ – still forecasted to rise 7% in FY24 – are a function of a challenging macroeconomic backdrop. If multiple compression is the result of handwringing regarding AI, it is a mistake. Certainly tasks such as writing and translation will be impacted, but those are typically jobs that cost less than $300 per task, which comprises less than 5% of Upwork’s GSV. That said, Upwork’s marketplace is comprised of more than ~10,000 skillsets, so the overall impact in the $300 and under space is limited. For now, AI is much more likely to augment human work than replace it.

As for activist Engine, management has plenty of cash flow to improve its platform to assuage obsolescence concerns and repurchase a significant number of shares. Trading at a PE on FY25E EPS of just over 10 and an EV/FY24E Adj. EBITDA of under 9, the valuations aren’t screaming ‘buy’, but in an industry that has only begun to be penetrated by online platforms such as Upwork’s, the upside potential outweighs the downside risk, making it a solid longer-term position. As such, I have initiated a small position in UPWK using covered call orders and will potentially accumulate a larger stake over time pending further developments.

data.title: ONEV: Not Really A Low-Volatility ETF
data.description:
ONEV strategy

The SPDR Russell 1000 Low Volatility Focus ETF (NYSEARCA:ONEV) was launched on 12/02/2015 and tracks the Russell 1000 Low Volatility Focused Factor Index. It has 430 holdings, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.61% and a total expense ratio of 0.20%.

As described by SSGA, the methodology selects and weights stocks of the Russell 1000 index based on "a combination of core factors (high value, high quality, and low size characteristics), with a focus factor comprising low volatility characteristics". The volatility score is based on the 5-year standard deviation of weekly total returns. The value score includes cash flow yield, earnings yield, and sales to price ratio. The quality score includes return on assets, change in asset turnover, accruals, and leverage. Constituents and industry weights are capped based on rules involving theirs weights in the parent index. The portfolio turnover rate in the most recent fiscal year was 34%. This article will use as a benchmark the parent index Russell 1000, represented by iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB).

Portfolio

The heaviest sectors in the portfolio are industrials (20.3%) and consumer discretionary (15.6%). Compared to the Russell 1000, ONEV overweights these two sectors, along with utilities, consumer staples and real estate. It massively underweights technology, communication and energy.

The portfolio is well-diversified and risks related to individual companies are low. Indeed, the top 10 holdings, listed below, represent 9.2% of asset value and the heaviest position weighs only 1.41%. In the Russell 1000, the top 10 issuers weigh over 30% in aggregate and the largest position is over 6%.

Performance

ONEV is the most diversified, but also the smallest and least liquid of these funds. The next chart compares total returns, starting on 9/13/2016 to match all inception dates. ONEV is in the middle of the pack, behind FDLO and LGLV, which have lower expense ratios.

ONEY comes in last position in 2024:

Takeaway

SPDR Russell 1000 Low Volatility Focus ETF implements a multi-factor strategy involving low volatility, value, quality and size. ONEV is better balanced across sectors and holdings than its parent index Russell 1000. It has value characteristics, but is unexpectedly slightly more volatile than the benchmark and has underperformed it since 2015. Moreover, ONEV has lagged two other low-volatility ETFs charging lower fees. FDLO, reviewed here, looks more attractive.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Stop Buying SPY And Start Buying XLG
data.description:
Pretty much everyone who invests in stocks is familiar with the S&P 500 (SP500).

The index is the most widely tracked equity index in the world, and it's comprised of the top 500 largest, most profitable public companies in the United States. For many, buying and holding stocks in the S&P 500 - typically through an ETF (NYSEARCA:SPY) - is the 'gold standard' of long-term investing.

When you invest into the S&P, your portfolio inherits a number of strengths, including broad diversification across sectors and the economies of scale that come with investing into large companies. The rebalancing aspect also makes sure that investors are consistently allocated to the best companies through time.

That said - what if you could skim the cream off the top?

What if you could get exposure to only the best of the best?

For us, this is where XLG, the Invesco S&P 500 Top 50 ETF (NYSEARCA:XLG), comes in. While the S&P 500 does a great job at diversifying capital into winners across sectors, XLG does a better job at focusing investor capital into the largest, most important, most established companies in the world.

In addition to the exponential advantages of scale that a company achieves as it grows, the largest companies are often the most well prepared to weather economic storms due to strong brands, balance sheets, and more.

As a result, in this article, we're making the case that XLG is a better place than the S&P 500 to invest your money over the next 10, 20, and 50 years.

Sound good?

Let's dive in.

Relative Outperformance

Let's start with the basics.

Most of you reading likely know how the S&P 500 works, so we'll skip talking about that in depth, but XLG may be a new instrument for some.

In short, instead of allocating capital to 500 different companies, XLG invests into the top 50 companies in the S&P 500. The fund is rebalanced on an annual basis, and re-weighted on a quarterly basis. As a market-cap weighted fund, there are no concentration limits and there's no 'diversification' policy.

Thus, the fund does hold outsized positions in only a few stocks, like Apple (AAPL), Nvidia (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and the like.

Similarly, the fund holds a much higher percentage of assets than the broader S&P 500 in the Tech (XLK) and Communication (XLC) sectors:

This concentration may first appear risky, but for the most part it has actually worked out to be a strategic advantage for investors, driving better returns with less risk vs. the S&P 500.

From the start of 2015, XLG has outperformed SPY by about 6,000 basis points:

This is impressive in and of itself, but if you zoom out to 2007, XLG has almost always been a better place to invest your capital at any given time:

This (abbreviated) table above shows the performance of a 'buy and hold' investment in SPY vs. a 'buy and hold' investment in XLG from 2007 through the present day. It shows how you would have done with a buy on any given week between then and now.

As the summary stats on the top right show, on a standalone basis, XLG would have been a better allocation (measured by returns through today) of capital on 892 of those weeks, vs. 16 weeks for the S&P 500.

This highlights how XLG's outperformance has been strong throughout cycles, no matter when in the cycle capital was allocated to the strategy.

At the same time, XLG's volatility has actually been slightly lower than the S&P over a similar span:

While the difference isn't huge, when you add it together with XLG's superior total historical returns, the compounding of the incremental advantages turn into a significantly improved risk/reward profile vs. the S&P 500 index.

Finally, the fund has achieved lower volatility during crucial periods for the market, like the Covid crash:

This table above shows S&P 500 and XLG weekly ATR during Covid, and as you can see XLG had lower volatility for most of the weeks during the crash and rebound.

While total returns on a week-to-week basis during Covid were similar to the market, the vol situation was better.

XLG Vs. SPY

There are a couple of reasons why XLG's market approach has produced better returns than the S&P 500 with less volatility over time.

Here are the key ones we thought we should highlight:

1.) Innovation Math - As the world gets more and more complex technologically, more and more upfront capital is required to design and produce cutting edge products.

As a result, the largest companies that have inbuilt stores of human and financial capital should have an advantage when it comes to generating shareholder value in the future.

In this case, we're talking about firms like Nvidia, Apple, Tesla (TSLA) and more.

This is tough to quantify, but going forward, we expect that the benefits of technological acceleration will accrue to a smaller and smaller number of firms. You're seeing this with AI, and as other innovations come down the pike - Quantum computing, Fusion, etc, this should accelerate.

It's easy to assume that things will always be the way they have always been, but as this chart shows, investing requires some level of vision as to where things could go:

As a result, XLG's approach of investing in leading, high margin companies should produce significant outperformance vs. the S&P, which is far less 'focused' on these world-beating firms.

Remember, the S&P 500 still invests more than 40% of its capital outside of these top 50 firms, including into lower margin, lower growth companies like United Airlines (UAL), Bunge (BG), and Campbell Soup Co (CPB).

2.) Scale advantages - If you look at capitalism as a game, then the largest public companies are the 'winners'.

Achieving the massive scale needed to make it to this level doesn't happen by accident, and as leading companies have grown larger, economies of scale unlock, which re-enforces a virtuous cycle.

In tech, companies like Google (GOOG) and Facebook (META) utilize large user bases to generate data that enhances algorithms and fuels innovation, while network effects make their platforms increasingly valuable as they grow.

In the consumer space, big manufacturers secure better supplier deals and invest in efficient production and marketing, reducing costs and increasing brand loyalty.

As we've argued in our articles on QQQ and XLK, the high margin Tech, Health, and Consumer companies of today have incredible scale that makes it hard for incumbents to gain a foothold.

3.) Economic Stability - Finally, when it comes to weathering downturns, large companies are uniquely well positioned to outperform.

On the financial front, many of the top firms have incredible balance sheets and robust gross margins which should allow them to adapt to adverse market conditions, and on the qualitative front, many of the firms that XLG invests in have such an omnipresence in their respective fields that they should benefit on a relative basis from somewhat inelastic revenue drivers.

All told, and taken together, large firms often have a competitive advantage that is difficult to dislodge for newcomers.

The S&P 500 uses these concepts - combined with rebalancing - to create a durable, investible vehicle.

Thus, it raises the question - why not do the same thing, but with an even greater degree of focus?

Risks

We like XLG here vs. SPY, but there are some risks to be aware of.

The key risk here for XLG investors, perhaps somewhat unintuitively, is breakup risk.

Normally, company breakups are good for investors and consumers. More management focus can be placed on maximizing a smaller segment from the broken-up company's TAM, and the removal of a monopoly position creates competition, choice, and ultimately lower prices for consumers.

On the investor side, many of the breakups in the late 1800's and early 1900's were incredibly accretive for shareholders over time, growing to multiples of the original company's valuation:

As a result, a breakup isn't always a bad thing for a huge company with a monopoly position.

Conversely, here, with XLG, if a company is broken up, then its' constituent parts may no longer be included the fund's portfolio, which means that XLG investors could miss out on gains that could come from this dynamic.

This isn't the case with SPY, which would likely 'catch' a number of these smaller spinoffs within it's '500 company' remit.

Recent news with Google has highlighted this risk, and it's something to think about going forward, in an era where Mag 7 companies wield an increasing amount of power.

Summary

Otherwise, though, we think XLG is a much better option than SPY for those seeking total returns from best-in-class companies with tremendous advantages.

With 50 stocks, investors can still enjoy the benefits of diversification with a higher degree of focus vs. the index, which is why we rate XLG a 'Buy'.

Consequently, we re-iterate our SPY rating of 'Hold'.

Cheers!

data.title: PIMCO CEF Update: Muni Coverage And The Premium Mystery
data.description:
In this article, we provide an update on the PIMCO CEF suite. Specifically, we discuss recent changes in distribution coverage. We also discuss the surprising increase in Muni fund coverage levels with our takeaway to not take it at face value.

Coverage Update

Based on six-month rolling figures, distribution coverage across the taxable and tax-exempt suites diverged over August. Coverage of the Muni suite continued to grow in a straight line while the average coverage level in the taxable suite fell back to 75%. Outside of PDO, which is the only taxable fund with coverage above 100%, the average taxable coverage is just north of 71%.

The tipping point for the sharp increase in Muni CEF coverage in the last few months appears to be the retirement of auction-rate preferreds or ARPS held by the CEFs. However, the sheer size of the coverage increase cannot be explained by ARPS alone, so this element remains somewhat of a mystery. Six-month coverage will likely continue to grow somewhat to match three-month coverage levels which are about 15% higher on average.

Until April of this year the Muni funds had two types of preferreds - the ARPS, i.e., auction-rate preferreds and the RVMTPs, i.e., variable-rate term preferreds. The ARPS were then retired and likely replaced with the RVMTPs. The RVMTPs have tended to carry a lower interest rate than the ARPS however not by enough to drive such a big increase in coverage.

Moreover, the implied level of net investment income implies an unrealistically low cost of leverage. For example, PMX has 126% coverage on a 6-month basis (3-month rolling coverage is even higher at 136%). This suggests it is generating net investment income of $0.50 annualized.

If we take a look at the semi-annual report ending Jun-24 we get net investment income of $0.38 or a quarter lower. We need to remember that this report would slightly overstate today's NII given the average leverage cost over the period of the report was less than leverage cost over August given where short-term rates are relative to 2023.

If we then subtract distributions to ARPS (ARPS distributions are not included in net investment income though RVMTPs are as the latter are liabilities due to having a maturity) we get net investment income of $0.24 or less than half of today's implied figure. The ARPS disappeared, and they were replaced with something else, most likely more RVMTPs. However, coverage is behaving as though they were replaced with something whose cost of leverage is negative which is obviously ludicrous.

What could allow a fixed-income fund to increase its distribution coverage? For one, it can lower its distribution. Two, its leverage cost could fall. Three, a fund could rotate into higher-coupon bonds. Four, it could take advantage of the shape of the yield curve such as moving into floating-rate securities as the yield curve remains inverted. Five, it could take significantly more credit risk and move into assets with wider credit spreads. Six, the rally in assets can lower leverage and free up capacity for additional assets and net income growth. None of these factors could sufficiently move the needle in a few months for the PIMCO Municipal funds to see such an increase in income so currently we are not taking the high coverage at face value.

Returns And Valuation

The taxable CEFs delivered returns mostly in the 10-15% range this year in total NAV terms. Discount tightening added to total price returns save for PHK.

PCM is the only fund with a drop in the NAV this year.

Total NAV returns of the Muni CEFs are not particularly exceptional. Discount tightening did much of the heavy lifting to drive sizable total price returns.

The average premium of the taxable suite is not far off 20% and is historically elevated.

The median twelve-month trailing distribution rate of the taxable suite is the lowest since mid-2022.

All the taxable funds are trading at a premium with RCS at a bonkers 72% level. It's not really clear what the gameplan for RCS shareholders is here at this valuation.

PIMCO Muni CEF valuations are all over the place. For some reason investors really like the trio of national CEFs PMX, PMF and PML which are trading well above the average valuation of the national Muni CEF sector. The NY and CA state funds trade at more reasonable valuations.

Typically, a CEF trades at a premium due to either above-average total NAV returns or above-average distribution rates. Historically, the three national Muni CEFs had above-average distribution rates however this changed recently as much of the sector significantly raised distributions to ward off investors interest. At this point, the distribution rates on price of the three funds are actually below average.

As far as historical returns, there is nothing here that supports a premium valuation either. The national CEFs have underperformed the sector over the last 3-5 years in total NAV terms and are flat to the sector over the last decade.

The main problem with the national trio, as we have discussed elsewhere, is that they tend to run at very elevated levels of leverage through the cycle, which often leads to a forced deleveraging and a lock-in of economic losses. In short, during sharp drawdowns the funds deleverage which prevents them from achieving a full recovery. We can see this dynamic below during the GFC if we compare PML to a Nuveen Muni CEF NAD.

And we saw it again in 2022 where PML continues to trail behind and will do so even if yields retrace all the way back.

In short, it's not clear what's supporting the premium valuations of the national CEF trio. We view them as not very good value and not very good funds at that.

Within the fixed-income taxable suite, we maintain our preference for PGP. The fund is leading the suite in total NAV terms since we highlighted it, and is second to RCS in total price terms since then. We also continue to like PDX, which has more of a hybrid allocation and Energy flavor and trades at an 8% discount.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kinder Morgan: Becoming A 'Dividend Aristocrat' With An Excessive Valuation Premium
data.description:
Kinder Morgan (NYSE:KMI) announced its Q3 earnings Wednesday afternoon with a small $0.02 EPS miss at $0.25 and a revenue miss of $360M, down 5.6% YoY. As of Thursday morning, the market reacted fine to that news, with KMI rising 1.1% at the open. On the positive side, its dividend is now $1.15 annualized, up 2% YoY, while its gross margin is up 7% from last year. Its forward guidance is essentially unchanged, with the company expecting to maintain its CapEx budget, leverage, and growth targets over the next year, reflecting a continued trend toward fewer acquisition and development projects and greater free cash flow.

Despite minor improvements to natural gas fundamentals in 2024, KMI has broken sharply to the upside, rising over 40% this year, with a staggering 16% rise over the past month. As a result, its forward dividend yield has compressed to 4.6%. Midstream MLP ETF (AMLP) has only increased by 10% this year. Although strong, the C-corp KMI has dramatically outperformed its primarily MLP competitors. Accordingly, KMI deserves closer analysis to determine if its breakout is founded on a growth or value opportunity or is based on excess investor speculation.

Natural Gas Midstream's "Stability Era"

Natural gas has become the dominant power source in the US. Since June, the fuel source has accounted for 46% of all US electricity supply as demand accelerates faster than expected, likely due to higher interest rates. Generally, higher borrowing costs promote natural gas generation, while renewables benefit from low rates due to higher upfront costs.

At any rate, the long-term "energy transition" trend is primarily from "dirty" fossil fuel sources like coal toward more efficient natural gas. This transition was extremely rapid during most of the 2000s and 2010s, with production growth slowing markedly since 2020. This is seen in both production data and the natural gas rig count, which indicates future production growth levels:

Although it may be surprising, the 2010s period was not ideal for most natural gas companies, such as producers and midstream firms. Midstream companies, like Kinder Morgan, profit from transporting natural gas through pipelines across North America. Although the 2010s saw immense expansion of the North American natural gas pipeline network, that created a tremendous glut in the broader fossil fuel market, leading to lower fee revenues and volume volatility. Further, the race to expand quickly led many, such as Kinder Morgan, to take on excessive leverage, often at abnormally high interest rates.

The slow-growth period since 2020 has led to maturity and stability for Kinder Morgan and most midstream firms, despite the sharp decline in natural gas prices since 2022. That is partly due to consistently higher US natural gas exports to Europe, offsetting lost Russian supplies. Given the state of the sabotaged Nord Stream Pipeline and global relations, I expect this to be a semi-permanent change that continues to benefit midstream firms.

Kinder Morgan is not solely a natural gas transportation company. That accounts for around 57% of its business, while 26% comes from refined products. The remaining split is between CO2 transportation (as a byproduct of extraction) and natural gas gathering and processing. Roughly 68% of its contracts are "take-or-pay," while 27% are fee-based. The remaining 5% are the riskier commodity-price-based contracts. "Take-or-pay" contracts are the most stable because they do not lose revenue if volumes decline; they're essentially "rent" for a specific capacity level. In contrast, fee-based revenues will rise and fall with fuel production and consumption levels.

The relationship between natural gas prices and midstream revenues is often misunderstood. Historically, this is a positive relationship wherein higher natural gas prices promote production growth, improving transportation demand. However, low natural gas can encourage more utilities to seek natural gas, increasing demand, even if that hampers supply growth. I believe we're now in the latter situation, as the natural gas supply is not growing in the US. Still, demand is due to higher interest rates, slowing renewables, and ultra-low natural gas prices.

Thus, the correlation between KMI's returns and natural gas, through the fund (UNG), waxes and wanes depending on market dynamics. The correlation has been positive since 2022, when natural gas export demand rose markedly, but has fallen toward zero since lower natural gas prices have only hampered supply growth, but not overall supply, and have likely further improved demand. See below:

I believe this situation may be the "new normal" for natural gas. The immense production growth period of the 2010s is firmly over, replaced by stability as natural gas becomes the dominant power source. I expect this trend will continue as coal continues to be phased out and potentially accelerated by nuclear power plant closures.

As Kinder Morgan points out, natural gas demand may rise due to AI improving electricity demand. I am not convinced of that, as AI currently uses just ~8 TWH, or around two-thousandths of the US's overall 4K TWH demand. I estimate that it would require a 100X increase in AI power consumption to materially increase electricity demand enough to generate notably higher revenues for KMI. That would take time, likely offset by efficiency improvements that lower electricity demand. For me, the aged nuclear power plants and efforts to reduce coal consumption are the most apparent positive catalysts for growth in natural gas consumption.

Of course, natural gas consumption can only rise as pipelines continue to be expanded. In recent years, we've seen increased pushback against pipeline construction, most recently in Tennessee's Kinder Morgan project. Although this directly hampers KMI's growth efforts and increases its costs due to regulatory requirements, I do not think anti-pipeline efforts are necessarily bearish for Kinder Morgan as they might increase market power.

Of course, the regulator FERC mandates minimum and maximum fees to limit KMI's ability to raise rates given a pipeline shortage; however, fees are still rising due to higher inflation, while FERC has arguably reduced the ability for shippers to contest rate increases by changing how it accounts for abnormal rates.

The impact is negligible, indicating higher fees likely account for more significant costs. KMI's operating income is relatively stable and is no longer rising. Despite reductions to its overall debt leverage, its total interest costs still absorb around 44% of its operating income. See below:

Importantly, because the market is slowing and stabilizing, Kinder Morgan is spending less on capital expenditures and growth, which often require external financing. As a result, its revenue per share has stagnated, but its cash flow per share is rising:

KMI is not an MLP, so it need not pay its cash flows out as dividends. Hypothetically, that may allow KMI more significant growth potential by reinvesting its dividends. Its dividend per share is around $1.14 annually, which could be doubled or more without compromising its cash position. That said, its depreciation is about $2.3B TTM or just over $1 per share. Realistically, the company cannot pay out all its cash flows as some of its income must go to maintaining its physical capital.

The Bottom Line

Compared to past years, I think it is best to see KMI as more of a dividend aristocrat than a growth company. There is still some potential for growth in the natural gas industry, but far less than there was two decades ago. The analyst consensus does not expect substantial revenue growth from KMI over the coming four years, with the most growth potential likely coming from creative acquisitions. That said, acquisitions can only improve KMI's income if they lower costs compared to sales, as its ability to exercise market power is limited by FERC. Still, its EPS is expected to rise to ~$1.60-$1.70 by the decade's end, giving it a long-term forward "P/E" of ~15X.

Though its sub 4% dividend yield is better than most stocks, it is lower than the 7.7% found in the midstream MLPs of (AMLP). Those firms must pay higher dividends; however, I think that points to KMI being potentially overvalued as it does not fundamentally differ significantly from its peers. KMI is more extensive but does not substantially improve its market power. In my view, its valuation premium may be justified by its greater ability to exercise economy of scale by reducing costs on acquired and developed pipelines. Yet, it has not significantly improved its margins or interest cost over recent years.

Although I believe KMI is roughly fairly valued, I think most investors are better off in other midstream investments with higher dividend yields. Energy Transfer (ET) has a 7.8% yield with nearly identical stock price volatility and size as KMI. Indeed, of the oil and gas transportation and storage companies with market capitalizations over $10B, KMI is among the most highly valued. Even then, many midstream stocks seem to rise due to the AI buzz around natural gas demand. To me, that is poorly grounded, given AI power use is still extremely low. Thus, I am neutral on KMI but see it as a decent long-term portfolio investment, with Energy Transfer likely a better bet.

data.title: International Consolidated Airlines Group Stock: Still A Buy, But Risk Of Plateauing Upside
data.description:
Airlines definitely do not make for the most attractive investments, especially not in the long term. At the same time, we do see many airline names in Europe and North America trade at levels that would almost give investors the impression that the pandemic is still not over. So, from a valuation perspective, I definitely do see opportunities.

One name that has performed well is International Airlines Group. The screenshot above shows the performance of IAG as part of the airline portfolio of The Aerospace Forum. While IAG is not the best performer in the portfolio, it definitely has an extremely appreciable performance profile and gained nearly 20% since my last coverage in May this year.

In this report, I will be discussing the most recent earnings of IAG, along with a risk assessment, and I will be assessing whether International Consolidated Airlines Group is a buy, sell or hold supplemented with a multi-year stock price target calculation.

IAG Benefits From Strength In Core Markets

During the second quarter, total revenues grew 7.8% to €8.3 billion driven by passenger growth of 9.9% on capacity expansion of 8% indicating expansion of unit revenues of 1.8%. At the same time, operating costs increased 9.5% indicating unit costs increasing by 1.4% and stable non-fuel costs. This was primarily driven by higher wages and a higher headcount and higher engineering and aircraft related costs, as well as an increase in depreciation and amortization. While cost grew in excess of capacity increases, I believe that with the inflationary pressures on labor in mind, the company managed cost rather well, posting operating profits of €1.24 billion, which was slightly lower year-on-year. Margins stood at a very strong 15% but were lower than the 16.3% seen in the second quarter of 2023.

Generally, we see that unit revenues are declining in all geographic markets with North America and Europe being the exception and those markets also are the core of IAG’s business where it was able to couple unit revenue expansion with higher capacity allocation. In Latin America and the Asia Pacific, we saw double-digit growth in capacity at a slight reduction in unit revenues. The Asia Pacific market is relatively small for IAG, accounting for 4% of the capacity, and generally that market segment is experiencing weak demand due to weak demand for travel to and from China. The domestic markets in Spain and the UK saw unit revenues decrease while capacity is being added, possibly indicating a slight imbalance or further normalization in unit revenues. In Africa, Middle East and South Asia, the capacity declined 0.6% and unit revenues declined 0.3% driven by the turmoil in the Middle East. At the same time, the Indian market is showing strength.

The strong performance in the first half of the year has led to a net debt to EBITDA of 1.1x, down from 1.7x at the start of the year, with net debt down to €6.4 billion from €9.2 billion. So, IAG is using its operational strength to decrease its leverage rather quickly, and it is now significantly below the 1.8x that the company targets. I do believe that part of the buffer will be used to fund capex, which could ultimately increase the leverage again.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For IAG?

The risks that IAG faces are largely the same as for other airlines and include inflationary cost pressures, although I do have to note that IAG has been doing a better job in moderating unit cost growth compared to many other airlines. Other risks include fuel price fluctuations, for which the airline group is using a fuel hedging strategy to catch off risk and unit revenue declines in the passenger and cargo business, as well as geopolitical and macroeconomic pressures as seen in the Middle East and China. A pressure specific to IAG is the capacity cap at Dublin Airport, which may force the IAG group to tweak its network to allow further expansion from Dublin Airport for Aer Lingus and grow more aggressively from it second hub in Manchester.

There also are opportunities for IAG as corporate travel has not fully recovered at British Airways with volumes recovered 65% and revenue recovery of 80% compared to 2019. Other parts of the group are showing different recovery profiles, with Iberia operating at 90% corporate volume and revenues exceeding 2019 levels and Aer Lingus being nearly fully recovered on volumes but with a 95% recovery in revenues.

IAG Stock Is Undervalued But We Do See Plateauing Upside

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For IAG, I have based the price target on the IAG listing at the London Stock Exchange converted to USD as the home listing provides better volumes allowing for better price making and buying and selling at desired prices and quantities. Other ways to invest in International Airlines Group are through the OTC tickers ICAGY (representing two ordinary shares) and BABWF (representing one ordinary share). EBITDA estimates have gone up by 1.9% with an increase of 3.8% for 2024, 2.8% for 2025 and 1% for 2026. At the same time, free cash flow expectations have increased by 43% to a free cash flow generation of $6.9 billion between 2023 and 2024. This increase is driven by continued delays in aircraft deliveries that drive the capital expenditures down. For IAG, I see 31% upside using a median company EV/EBITDA, which would result in a price target of $3.62. At the same time, we do note that upside in the years after is not compelling using the median EV/EBITDA. That means that if EV/EBITDA multiples for IAG do not expand towards the level of the peer group, beyond the 31% upside currently envisioned, there will be little upside.

Conclusion: IAG Is A Strong European Airline Group

While operating profits plateaued, the results also put on display a prudent management strategy with an appreciable exposure to the markets that show strength. That's definitely not the case for all airline groups at this point. Furthermore, what I do like is that the company’s gross debt as well as net debt continue to decrease, and the leverage is well below the targeted multiple of 1.8. I believe that shows how the company has used the end market strength to improve its balance sheet. For the time being, I do believe that the stock remains a buy, but do note that beyond the 31% upside that I currently do see for the stock, additional upside can only be unlocked on the basis of EV/EBITDA expansion towards peer group levels.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: TSMC's Q3 2024 Earnings Beat Unveils A New Catalyst
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited aka TSMC (NYSE:TSM) delivered a blockbuster quarter that met high expectations following the release of preliminary sales data earlier this month, which outperformed expectations. The company's third quarter results and forward outlook continue to highlight robust AI demand, putting earlier investors' concerns about AI fatigue back to rest. TSMC's Q3 results have also overshadowed risks of a diminishing smartphone sales mix amid softer-than-expected demand for its largest customer, Apple Inc.'s (AAPL), newest AI-enabled iPhone 16s. And despite an increasing mix shift towards more advanced nodes to support AI deployments, particularly the 3nm ("N3") process which remains margin dilutive, TSMC also outperformed its earlier gross margin guidance by more than two points to 57.8%.

TSMC's latest streak of growth and earnings outperformance reinforces our view that its upcoming start of volume productions on the 2nm process node represents an impending catalyst for the stock. Specifically, the next-generation process node delivers better unit economics and is expected to drive further margin accretion for TSMC. Demand is also underpinned by ongoing power efficiency requirements to support increasingly complex and compute-intensive AI workloads, which can be addressed by the next-generation node. Taken together, TSMC's upcoming entry into 2nm, combined with its proprietary advanced CoWoS packaging technology's mission-critical role in AI developments, underscores an impending upward re-rate for the stock from current levels.

An Accelerating AI Opportunity

TSMC's moat continues to come from its advanced chip manufacturing and packaging capabilities at scale, especially amid heightened demand for complex, compute-intensive AI hardware. In the latest earnings update, management has reiterated their optimism for a 10% y/y expansion in the overall semiconductor market, ex-memory. Investors are also reminded of TSMC's redefined "Foundry 2.0" roadmap, which will expand from chip manufacturing to include "packaging, testing, mask making, and others and all IDM, excluding memory manufacturing." This continues to reflect TSMC's expanding TAM and revenue opportunity, which management has quantified during both the 2Q and 3Q earnings call:

Under this new definition [of Foundry 2.0], the size of the foundry industry was close to $250 billion in 2023, as compared to $115 billion under the previous definition…TSMC's share of the foundry industry, under our new definition, was 28% in 2023, supported by our strong technology leadership and broader customer base, we expect this one to further increase in 2024.

Source: TSMC 2Q24 Earnings Call Transcript.

Let me assure you that the last time we proposed a new version of the Foundry 2.0, which including the wafer manufacturing and packaging and testing, mass making, and all others. All these kinds of things become more growing importance, like packaging, testing, mass making….And so I think Foundry 2.0 is a better reflect TSMC's traceable market. And our share is probably around 30%, so not a dominant yet.

Source: TSMC 3Q24 Earnings Call Transcript.

The robust demand environment is further corroborated by key customer Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) latest commentary during its "Advancing AI" innovative keynote. Specifically, AMD predicts the AI processor marketing alone to advance at a 60% CAGR between 2023 and 2028 to represent a $500 billion opportunity. This continues to underpin TSMC's tremendous growth prospects, given that "almost all AI innovators" are collaborating with the company on next-generation processors.

Meanwhile, earlier worries about AI spending fatigue have also been debunked amid resilient end-market demand for both accelerators and data center CPUs, which are highly dependent on TSMC's advanced technologies. Hyperscalers have earmarked more than $160 billion in capex towards AI initiatives this year, with further headroom for longer-term growth given the accelerating pace of global data center expansion. And TSMC continues to be a key beneficiary given its technology moat, as evidenced in the increasing mix shift towards HPC sales amid an accelerating growth trajectory.

Recent endorsement by key chipmakers, NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and AMD, also reinforces TSMC's unmatched advantage against emerging fabrication rivals like Intel Corporation (INTC) by wide margins:

We're fabbing at a TSMC because it's the world's best and it's the world's best not by a small margin, it's the world's best by an incredible margin…And so we use them because they're great, but if necessary, of course, we can always bring up others.

Source: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, Goldman Sachs Communacopia + Technology Conference Transcript.

TSMC is a fantastic partner. I mean they have been an excellent partner to us across all of the various aspects of technology and manufacturing…We're happy that TSMC is building Arizona. We're taping out products and ramping that. And we'll continue to look at how to derisk the supply chain with the notion of this is an industry-wide problem and all of us are looking at how do we create just more geographic diversity.

Source: AMD CEO Lisa Su, Goldman Sachs Communacopia + Technology Conference Transcript.

Admittedly, geopolitical risks remain a significant immediate overhang on the stock, given its saturated manufacturing footprint in Taiwan. However, continued diversification of its overseas operations, with the Arizona and Kumamoto plants coming online next year, is expected to mitigate said risks partially.

In the latest development, AMD has qualified chip production at TSMC's upcoming Arizona facility. Although details remain limited, the industry is reporting that AMD's upcoming Instinct MI325X AI accelerators might be produced in Arizona, which supports the 4nm process node. If true, this will provide substantial validation to TSMC's advanced manufacturing capabilities overseas, and effectively reduce the risk premium currently attributable to its elevated exposure to China-Taiwan tensions.

2nm is an Impending Catalyst for TSMC

Recall that the 3nm node has largely been margin dilutive for TSMC since its debut, despite robust adoption in the midst of relentless AI hardware requirements. This was largely due to the underpricing of the advanced node during the early stages of its tape-out and customer contracting, which did not account for subsequent implications of inflationary pressures recently.

Yet, TSMC's margins have been expanding at a rapid pace, despite the increasing mix shift towards the 3nm process node alongside rising electricity costs in Taiwan. The company delivered a substantial Q3 earnings beat, with gross margins exceeding the upper range of previous guidance by more than two percentage points at 57.8%. Paired with additional cost savings realized through improved utilization and increased productivity gains, TSMC's latest results set a strong foundation for additional margin accretion from upcoming volumes on 2nm (or "N2").

As a result, we believe TSMC's upcoming start of volume production on the 2nm process node will be a key catalyst for the stock. The next-generation process node is expected to deliver superior unit economics when compared to its predecessors, 3nm and 5nm, and will be complemented by a faster ramp schedule. It also benefits from a strong demand outlook, given N2's substantial lead in power efficiencies compared to its predecessors. This effectively addresses a critical consideration in the development of next-generation compute-intensive AI hardware.

N2 Unit Economics

Unlike its predecessor, the N2 process node will be better priced to its value according to management. Although management has yet to provide details on N2 pricing, the industry expects it to "exceed $30,000 per wafer." This represents a marked increase from the N3's ~$20,000 per wafer and almost double from the 4nm and 5nm process nodes.

In addition to pricing, N2 will also benefit from higher tape-outs, which underscores a stronger growth outlook. Specifically, management expects new tape-outs - or design wins proceeding to manufacturing - in the first two years of N2 production to be higher than volumes observed from the 3nm and 5nm processes over the same timeframe. The emerging technology's performance and yield are also already ahead of plan, preserving TSMC's competitive moat against up-and-coming rivals in advanced nodes, such as Intel's 18A.

Taken together, the N2 is expected to be a key impending margin accretive factor for TSMC. Compared to the 5nm and 3nm process nodes, which took about 10 to 12 quarters to become margin accretive for TSMC, the N2's better pricing and yield are expected to deliver a substantially better profitability profile at a much faster pace. This is supportive of an upward re-rate to TSMC's fundamental trajectory, which remains underappreciated at the stock's current levels.

N2 Demand Outlook

The materialization of N2's favorable unit economics is further reinforced by a strong demand outlook for the technology. Specifically, the ongoing AI transformation has unleashed an "insatiable need for energy-efficient computing." This is corroborated by Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang's latest remarks, which state that computing costs will have to come down substantially to make next-generation AI developments, such as "reasoning," feasible. The leader of AI processors expects to "boost its chip performance every year by two to three times," while maintaining minimal changes to cost and energy consumption levels.

And TSMC's next-generation advanced node can address these requirements. Specifically, TSMC's N2 node will incorporate a "nanosheet transistors structure", which unlocks industry-leading density and energy efficiency. The N2 will deliver "10 to 15 speed improvement at the same power, or 25% to 30% power improvement at the same speed, and more than 15% chip density increase as compared with the N3E".

This performance and power advantage will be furthered by the N2P extension coming 2H26 - similar to the current N3's ramp profile, with extensions N3X, N3P and N3E - which incorporates "backside power delivery". The N2P will deliver an incremental 10% power benefit on top of the N2 node.

The N2 family's leading performance capabilities and power efficiency will be succeeded by further advancements in the A16 node, which starts volume production in 2H26 as well. The A16 node will be the first to feature TSMC's proprietary "Super Power Rail" ("SPR") backside power delivery technology. The composition will unlock up to 10% power improvement at the same speed as N2P, which represents power improvement of up to 20%. It is also 7% to 10% denser, which enables greater performance while maintaining lower power consumption.

To better address robust demand for its advanced nodes, driven by the ongoing AI transformation's requirements for power-efficient computing, TSMC has also invested accordingly in capacity expansion. The company's second Arizona fab will be fitted with 2nm manufacturing technologies, and start volume production by 2028 to complement output from its Taiwan facilities. This will be complemented by additional capacity for more advanced technologies at its third fab in the U.S., which is expected to come online by the end of the decade.

Fundamental Considerations

Adjusting our previous forecast for TSMC's actual Q3 performance and forward outlook, we expect its full-year 2024 revenue to expand 29% y/y to $89.5 billion. We expect a continued mix shift towards HPC, given TSMC's industry-leading role in the production of AI processors, to facilitate the ongoing upgrade cycle in data center builds.

Despite the increasing mix shift towards 3nm, we expect continued moderation in relevant margin dilution due to expanding scale, complemented by ongoing manufacturing cost optimization efforts. This is consistent with TSMC's blockbuster earnings outperformance observed in Q3, and management's guidance to further margin expansion heading into the end of the year. Paired with expectations for further margin accretion given better unit economics from next-generation process nodes, we expect TSMC to outperform its long-term gross margin target of more than 53%.

Valuation Considerations

We expect the upcoming start of volume productions of N2 to be an impending catalyst for the TSMC stock, especially given the company's streak of substantial earnings outperformance since introducing N3. The new N2 volumes are expected to drive further margin accretion, benefitting cash flows that underpin TSMC's valuation outlook. As a result, we are increasing our price target for the stock to $212 (previously $180).

The price is derived using the discounted cash flow analysis, which considers projections taken from the foregoing fundamental discussion. A WACC of 10.3% in line with TSMC's capital structure and risk profile is applied. The analysis also considers an implied perpetual growth rate of 1.5% on 2033E EBITDA (or 6.3% on 2028E EBITDA) to determine TSMC's terminal value at steady-state. We believe the valuation assumption applied, which gears towards the lower range of the anticipated rate of long-term economic expansion across its core operating regions, appropriately accounts for TSMC's incremental geopolitical risk premium. The analysis also anticipates a capex spend of $28 billion in 2024, or 31% of the current year's revenue estimate, to support the company's long-term growth expectations.

Conclusion

Given TSMC's growth outlook, we believe the stock continues to trade discounted on a relative basis to its chip-making peers with similar fundamental profiles. There is substantial upside for TSMC from current levels, especially given its competitive advantage in leading-edge manufacturing and advanced packaging technologies. All AI chipmakers are currently working with TSMC, as it remains the preferred chip fab despite inherent geopolitical risks. This continues to underscore that the industry has yet to find a close replacement to TSMC's technological capabilities and scale.

Although geopolitical risks remain TSMC's most significant overhang, the impending start of volume production at its Arizona and Kumamoto fabs should provide some alleviation. At Arizona, specifically, TSMC is expected to start producing on the 4nm process node, with volumes already accounted for by Apple and AMD. Further expansion in the region will add diversification to its supply of advanced node manufacturing, such as 2nm, which is critical for addressing next-generation accelerated computing builds.

Taken together with better unit economics from its next-generation process nodes, TSMC is well-positioned for further margin accretion. This is expected to unlock an incremental upward re-rate to TSMC's long-term fundamental trajectory, which remains underappreciated at the stock's current levels.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Plumas Bancorp: Loan Growth May Offset Net Interest Margin Compression
data.description:
Introduction

Plumas Bancorp (NASDAQ:PLBC) has substantially underperformed the iShares U.S. Financials ETF (IYF) so far in 2024, delivering a ~3% gain versus the ~29% total return for the broad US financials ETF:

While the bank's 89% exposure to net interest income /NII/ creates significant challenges against the backdrop of lower Fed rates, Plumas Bancorp also boasts a strong CET1 ratio, which will allow it to sustain loan growth in the years ahead. This in turn should cushion the impact of Fed rate cuts and allow the bank to limit its profitability decline.

Considering my $3.50-4.00/share EPS estimate following Fed policy normalization, the bank trades at an attractive P/E multiple relative to the other US financials. As such, I rank it a Buy.

Company Overview

You can access all company results on the bank's website here. Plumas Bancorp is a regional bank focused on Northern California, Nevada, and Oregon. The loan portfolio is heavily concentrated in commercial real estate /CRE/, split between CRE/Investor at 26% of loans, CRE/Owner at 26%, and CRE/Multifamily at 9%. Agricultural loans make up 12% of its loan portfolio:

NII accounts for 89% of revenues, leaving just 11% for non-interest commission income, highlighting the bank's elevated exposure to interest rates and Fed policy.

Operational Overview

In Q3 2024 Plumas Bancorp reported EPS of $1.31/share, down 2% Y/Y as operating expenses were impacted by a non-recurring litigation charge. Loan growth remained robust at 5% Y/Y, with loans reaching $1 billion. As expected, growth was concentrated in the company's CRE portfolio.

Deposit developments were less favorable as total deposits declined 3.6% Y/Y to $1.35 billion. The silver lining is that deposits increased on a quarterly basis.

The investment securities portfolio was up 4.1% Y/Y to $457 million and is classified as available for sale, indicating the $21 million in unrealized losses are already reflected in equity.

Credit quality remained robust as non-performing assets only accounted for 0.29% of all assets, or $4.8 million.

Capital Position

Tangible shareholders' equity totaled $176 million at the end of Q3, 2024, or $29.78/share. The bank remains exceptionally well capitalized, with a CET1 ratio of 16.9%. For reference, the Basel III framework only requires banks to hold a CET1 ratio of 6.5% to be considered well-capitalized.

Even so, the payout policy remains very conservative, with the quarterly dividend set at $0.27/share, or 21% of Q3 2024 earnings.

Effect of Fed policy

CEO Andrew Ryback noted that lower Fed rates should help in retaining customer deposits:

“We anticipate increased deposit retention as a result of the Fed's decision to cut rates by 50bp in the third quarter as rate seeking funds movement activity will likely be tempered. Additionally, lower rates will likely ease potential challenges for variable rate borrowers.”

Even so, given that the bank paid an average of 0.97% on interest-bearing deposits in Q3 2024, I think deposits will remain under pressure since the Fed is only likely to cut interest rates to about 2.9% in the long term. The average deposit balance stood at $34 000, indicating no deposit concentration:

We also observe that 52% of deposits are non-interest bearing, which on the one hand drives profitability given robust lending margins, but on the other hand exposes the bank to continued deposit outflows.

Turning to the bank's loan portfolio, 77% of loans are floating rate, indicating significant negative effects from future Fed rate cuts. However, only 22% of these floating-rate loans are repriced within one day to three months of a change in the prime rate. Another 75% of floating rate loans reprice every five years.

Valuation and prospects

I expect the real estate market to stage a recovery as the Fed gradually lowers interest rates. Given the bank's CET1 ratio of 16.9%, Plumas Bancorp is well-positioned to finance new real estate developments and expand its CRE portfolio.

Against this backdrop of credit growth, the bank's net interest margin, which stood at 4.76% in Q3 2024, will likely decline going forward as its floating rate portfolio reprices to reflect lower benchmark interest rates. While I do not expect a return to the 3.63% margin observed in 2021, a net interest margin close to 4% is quite likely:

In the end, the net effect on profitability will depend on whether Plumas Bancorp can grow its loan portfolio sufficiently to offset the effect of lower interest rates.

From a valuation perspective, the bank trades at a 39% premium to its tangible book value, which is quite high and certainly does not signal any distress in its valuation. From a profitability standpoint, there is no reason to believe the bank cannot deliver at least $3.5-4.00/share in annual earnings, even if we factor in lower NII and stabilizing provisions. This puts the P/E ratio marginally above 10 which is attractive in itself. For reference, the IYF US financials ETF trades at a P/E ratio of about 16.7. As such, I think a Buy rating is warranted for the shares.

Risks

The main risk facing Plumas Bancorp is a US recession, which would prompt the Fed to cut rates below its 2.9% neutral rate. This in turn would severely impact NII given the bank's 77% floating-rate exposure.

Likewise, a potential recession would likely cause a deterioration in credit quality, which currently is very robust. However, given the bank's supreme capital position, this is unlikely to impact credit growth.

I would also flag that 52% of deposits are non-interest-bearing. This creates a funding risk as even after the Fed lowers interest rates, risk-free rates will remain substantially above 0%.

Conclusion

Plumas Bancorp's Q3 2024 EPS was impacted by a non-recurring legal expense. The bank remains well-capitalized and is growing its loan portfolio. I think the focus going forward will be on:

  • Whether lower interest rates cause an acceleration in credit demand and loan growth.
  • Whether lower interest rates bring a stabilization in deposits, as management expects.
  • Whether the bank maintains its robust underwriting standards as it expands its loan portfolio.

On the one hand, Plumas Bancorp is quite expensive given its 39% tangible book premium. On the other hand, the bank's strong capital position can sustain a substantially larger loan portfolio, which should cushion the impact from lower interest rates. As such, even after the Fed reaches its 2.9% neutral rate, I expect the bank to generate earnings of about $3.50-4.00/share. Given the current valuation, I think the stock is worth a Buy rating, as it trades at a significant discount to the US financials sector.

Thank you for reading.
